items.question: What chart type answers "how many examples per class" in a classification dataset?
items.answer: A bar chart of class counts (or proportions). The question compares discrete categories; bars encode magnitude along a common baseline better than pie slices or lines between unordered labels.

items.question: You have train_loss and val_loss per epoch. Which chart family and why?
items.answer: Line chart with epoch on the x-axis and loss on the y-axis, two labeled series. Epoch is an ordered index and the question is how metrics trend over training—not categorical comparison.

items.question: What does this topic own vs Matplotlib and Seaborn?
items.answer: This topic owns the decision framework—which of bar, line, scatter, histogram, box, or heatmap fits the question. Matplotlib owns drawing primitives and Figure/Axes; Seaborn owns statistical plotting wrappers. Decide here, implement there.

items.question: When is a box plot preferable to a bar chart of means?
items.answer: When the question is how a numeric variable's full distribution—not just its mean—differs across categories, such as latency spread across model versions or feature values across classes. Box plots show median, quartiles, and outliers; bars hide spread.

items.question: How does a correlation heatmap relate to scatter plots in an EDA workflow?
items.answer: Heatmap on df.corr() gives a matrix overview to flag strong linear pairs quickly. Scatter plots on selected pairs confirm shape and nonlinear effects. Heatmap screens; scatter diagnoses—both chart families, different questions.
